cards.case_title: VIVEK NARAYAN SHARMA v. UNION OF INDIA
cards.court: S. ABDUL NAZEER, B.R. GAVAI, A.S. BOPANNA, V. RAMASUBRAMANIAN AND B. V. NAGARATHNA, JJ.
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the power available to the Central Government under sub-section (2) of s.26 of the Reserve Bank of India Act can be restricted to mean that it can be exercised only for 'one' or 'some' series of bank notes and not for 'all' series of bank notes.
cards.holding: The power available to the Central Government under sub-section (2) of s.26 of the RBI Act cannot be restricted to mean that it can be exercised only for 'one' or 'some' series of bank notes and not for 'all' series of bank notes. The power can be exercised for all series of bank notes.
key_principles: The power available to the Central Government under sub-section (2) of s.26 of the RBI Act cannot be restricted to mean that it can be exercised only for 'one' or 'some' series of bank notes and not for 'all' series of bank notes.

Merely because on two earlier occasions, the demonetization exercise was by plenary legislation, it cannot be held that such a power would not be available to the Central Government under sub-section (2) of s.26 of the RBI Act.
cards.searchable_summary: The judgment discusses the interpretation of sub-section (2) of s.26 of the Reserve Bank of India Act and clarifies that the central government has the power to initiate and carry out demonetization for all series of bank notes, regardless of previous legislative actions.

cards.case_title: Jindal Stainless TTD. & Anr. v. State of Haryana & Ors.
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the imposition of compensatory tax on goods entering a local area for use, sale or consumption therein violates Article 304(a) of the Constitution.
cards.holding: The Compensatory Tax theory evolved in Automobile Transport case and subsequently modified in Jindal case has no juristic basis and is rejected. The question whether the taxes in the present case satisfy this test is left to be determined by the regular benches hearing the matter.
key_principles: Article 301
Article 304(a)
cards.searchable_summary: The Supreme Court held that compensatory tax violates Article 304(a), and whether it does so in this case is left to be determined by regular benches. The court also ruled out the applicability of the Compensatory Tax theory.

cards.case_title: P. M. ABUBAKAR v. STATE OF KARNATAKA AND ORS.
cards.court: (ANIL R. DAVE AND A. M. KHANWILKAR, JJ.)
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the order confirming the auction sale of a mortgaged property is appealable under Section 89A of the Karnataka Cooperative Societies Act, 1959 and Rules made thereunder.
cards.holding: We allow the appeals preferred by the auction purchaser. The order passed by the Deputy Registrar (dated 18.7.2009); and of the High Court(dated 11.01.2010; 24.8.2011, 8.6.2012, and 29.6.2012) confirming the order of the Deputy Registrar of setting aside the sale of the sub is not appealable as it is functus officio.
key_principles: Section 89A of the Karnataka Cooperative Societies Act, 1959
cards.searchable_summary: The case involves an auction sale of a mortgaged property under the Karnataka Cooperative Societies Act. The court held that the order confirming the sale is functus officio and not appealable.

cards.case_title: THE ADDITIONAL COMMISSIONER OF COMMERCIAL TAXES, BANGALORE v. A YILI STONE INDUSTRIES ETC. ETC.
cards.core_legal_question: Whether polished granite stone or tiles fall under separate entries in the Second Schedule of the Karnataka Sales Tax Act, 1957.
holding: There is a distinction between polished granite stone and tiles. If a polished granite stone is used in a building for any purpose, it will come under Entry 17(i) of Part S of the second schedule, but if it is a tile, which comes into existence by different process, a new and distinct commodity emerges.
A finding has to be arrived at by carrying out due enquiry, without which, a final conclusion cannot be reached.
key_principles: The distinction between polished granite stone and tiles based on the manufacturing process
cards.searchable_summary: In this case, the court held that polished granite stones fall under Entry 17(i) of Part S in the Second Schedule of the Karnataka Sales Tax Act, 1957. However, tiles are considered a distinct commodity due to their different production processes.

cards.case_title: BHUSHAN POWER & STEEL LIMITED v. MR. S.L. SEAL ADDL. SECRETARY (STEEL & MINES) GOVERNMENT OF ODISHA & ORS.
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the State Government had fulfilled its obligation to recommend the case to the Central Government for allotment of mining areas as per the Supreme Court's earlier directions.
cards.holding: The role of the State Government was only to send recommendations to the Central Government. The ultimate authority/power was vested with the Central Government to take a decision on the request of the State Government. Since the State Government did not act promptly and sent the necessary letter as recommended, the Supreme Court directed them to do so.
key_principles: The role of the State Government is advisory in nature when it comes to recommending cases for the Central Government's decision-making process.
The ultimate authority lies with the Central Government in matters related to mining area allotment.
cards.searchable_summary: Contempt petition filed by BHUSHAN POWER & STEEL LIMITED against the State Government of Odisha. The Supreme Court held that the State Government was only required to recommend cases for the Central Government's decision, and failed to do so promptly.

cards.case_title: IN RE: THE PUNJAB TERMINATION OF AGREEMENT ACT, A 2004 (Special Reference No. I of 2004)
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the Punjab Termination of Agreements Act, 2004 is valid and enforceable in light of a previous Supreme Court decree.
cards.holding: The Punjab Termination of Agreements Act, 2004 cannot be considered to be legal and valid. A party to the litigation cannot unilaterally act in a manner to nullify the effect of the decree passed by a court of law. The 1981 Agreement was about sharing of waters of Ravi and Beas rivers and thus could not have been unilaterally terminated by one of the parties to the agreement.
key_principles: A party to the litigation cannot unilaterally act in a manner to nullify the effect of the decree passed by a court of law.
A litigating person cannot become judge in its own cause.
The 1981 Agreement was about sharing of waters of Ravi and Beas rivers and thus could not have been unilaterally terminated by one of the parties to the agreement.
cards.searchable_summary: Supreme Court held that Punjab Termination of Agreements Act, 2004 is invalid due to its unilateral termination of a previous court decree. The act also nullified earlier decrees related to water sharing agreements.

cards.case_title: AHMEDABAD MUNICIPAL CORPORATION v. GTL INFRASTRUCTURE LTD. & ORS.
cards.key_legal_question: Whether mobile towers come within the fold of 'land and building' appearing in Entry 49 List II of the Seventh Schedule.
cards.holding: Mobile towers comes within the fold of 'land and building' appearing in Entry 49 List II of the Seventh Schedule - Power of taxation on mobile tower is vested in the State Legislature under Entry 49 of List II of the Seventh Schedule - Tax would be payable by the occupier and not the owner of the land and building.
key_principles: Taxation power on mobile towers lies with the State Legislature as per Entry 49, List II of the Seventh Schedule.
The occupier rather than the owner of the land and building is liable to pay property tax for mobile towers.
cards.searchable_summary: Supreme Court held that mobile towers are considered 'land and buildings' under Gujarat's Municipal Corporations Act. The court ruled that such structures fall within the State Legislature's power to levy taxes, and the occupier rather than the owner is responsible for paying property tax.

cards.case_title: MAHARAO BHIM SINGH OF KOTA v. COMMISSIONER OF INCOME-TAX, RAJASTHAN-11, JAIPUR
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the Ruler is entitled to claim exemption for his entire residential palace under Section 10(19A) of the Income Tax Act, 1961 despite letting out a part of it.
cards.holding: Even if the Ruler had let out a portion of his residential palace, he would continue to enjoy the exemption in respect of the entire palace because it is not possible to split the exemption. The Legislature did not intend to divide the exemption into two parts-one for the occupied portion and another for the tenant's possession.
key_principles: Exemption under Section 10(19A) applies to the entire residential palace of the Ruler, regardless of occupation by a tenant.
The Legislature must use specific language to indicate that exemptions should be split between different parts of the property.
cards.searchable_summary: In this case, the Supreme Court held that the Ruler is entitled to claim exemption for his entire residential palace under Section 10(19A) despite letting out a part of it. The court emphasized that splitting the exemption was not intended by the Legislature.

cards.case_title: LALITESHWAR PRASAD SINGH & ORS. v. S. P. SRIVASTAVA (D) THR. LRS.
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the High Court erred in not giving the appellants an opportunity to adduce evidence and make submissions during the first appeal against the suit for declaration of title.
holding: The Supreme Court held that all appellate courts are the final court of facts, and they must state the points for determination, the decision thereon, and the reasons for such decisions.
When an appellate court reverses the findings of the trial court, it must record these findings in clear terms explaining how the reasoning of the trial court was erroneous.
key_principles: Duty of appellate courts to state points for determination
cards.searchable_summary: The High Court's judgment in the appeal from a suit for declaration of title was set aside due to its failure to consider both parties' evidence and reasons, leading to an error in decision-making.

cards.case_title: RAMESH VERMA (D) TR. LRS. v. LAJESH SAXENA (D) BY LRS & ANR.
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the High Court was right in holding that females have the right to seek partition and divide the share of property left behind by their father under the Hindu Succession Act, 1956.
cards.holding: The Supreme Court held that after the enactment of the Hindu Succession Act, 1956, devolution of such property would be only by succession and not by survivorship. The High Court's decision was therefore incorrect in this regard.
key_principles: Hindu Succession Act, 1956
cards.searchable_summary: The case dealt with the applicability of the Hindu Succession Act, 1956 to a suit for partition of property. The court held that after the enactment of the act, females have no right to seek partition and divide the share of their father's property.

cards.case_title: JORSINGH GOVIND VANJARI v. DIVISIONAL CONTROLLER MAHARASHTRA, STATE ROAD TRANSPORT CORPORATION, JALGAON DIVISION
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the High Court was justified in awarding compensation to the appellant despite finding termination unjustified and not possible due to superannuation.
holding: The Supreme Court held that the Labour court's decision to grant compensation as a result of the perverse finding by the inquiry officer is valid, given that no other evidence could be provided by the employer to establish otherwise.
The High Court was also justified in awarding one-time compensation equivalent to 50% of the back wages without granting gratuity.
key_principles: termination not possible due to superannuation; denial of gratuity requires termination constituting an offence involving moral turpitude
cards.searchable_summary: The case involves a bus conductor who was terminated for misconduct but reinstatement was not possible due to his age. The Supreme Court upheld the Labour court's decision to award compensation, despite finding termination unjustified.

cards.case_title: Mahanagar Telephone Nigam Ltd. v. M/S Applied Electronics Ltd.
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the Code of Civil Procedure (CPC) can be applied in appeals under the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996.
cards.holding: The Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996 is a complete code that restricts challenges to awards and does not permit a second appeal. Therefore, no other provision, including CPC, can be attracted in such appeals.
key_principles: Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996 provides restrictions for challenging the award.
The Arbitration Act, 1940 has different provisions regarding the applicability of CPC to appeals.
cards.searchable_summary: In this judgment, the court held that the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996 restricts challenges to awards and does not permit a second appeal. The court distinguished it from the Arbitration Act, 1940.

cards.case_title: K. BALU & ANR. v. THE STATE OF TAMILNADU REP. BY ITS SECRETARY
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the court must accept the considered view formed by expert body based on statistics and data which made out a consistent pattern year after year.
cards.holding: The Court must accept the considered view formed by the expert body as same was based on statistics and data which made out a consistent pattern year after year. The existence of liquor vends, advertisements alluding to the availability of liquor coupled with arduous drives particularly in heavy vehicles makes it abundantly necessary to enforce the policy of the Union Government to safeguard human life.
key_principles: Public health as per Article 47 of the Constitution of India
cards.searchable_summary: The judgment held that the court must accept expert views based on consistent statistics, which is essential for enforcing a national government's policy aimed at reducing drink and drive accidents by prohibiting liquor sales along highways.

cards.case_title: Kedar Nath Yadav v. State of West Bengal & Ors.
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the acquisition by the State Industrial Development Corporation (SIDC) for an industrial project of a particular company falls under 'public purpose' as required by the Land Acquisition Act, 1894.
cards.holding: The Court held that since the procedure under Part VII of the Land Acquisition Act was not followed in the present case, the acquisition is grossly perverse, illegal and void ab initio. The objections filed under Section 5-A were rejected without assigning any clear reference to lay out plans or other connected records.
key_principles: Procedure under Part VII of the Land Acquisition Act must be followed for acquisitions by State Industrial Development Corporations (SIDC)
cards.searchable_summary: The Court found that the acquisition was not in 'public purpose' as required by law and thus held it to be illegal and void. The procedure under Part VII, which is applicable when acquiring land for a company's project, was not followed.

cards.case_title: MIS MADRAS PETROCHEM LTD. & ANR v. BIFR& ORS.
cards.court: KURIAN JOSEPH AND R.F. NARIMAN, JJ.
key_statutes: Securitisation and Reconstruction of Financial Assets and Enforcement of Security Interest Act, 2002 (SARFAESI Act)
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the SARFAESI Act prevails over the SICA to the extent of inconsistency therewith.
cards.holding: The SARFAESI Act prevails over the SICA to the extent of inconsistency. Where a secured creditor of a sick industrial company seeks to recover its debt in the manner provided by s.13 (2) of the SARFAESI Act, such secured creditor may realize such secured debt under s. 13(4) of the SARFAESI Act, notwithstanding the provisions of s.22 of SICA.
key_principles: SARFAESI Act prevails over SICA to the extent of inconsistency.

Where there are more than one secured creditor or jointly financed by secured creditors, at least 60% in value of the amount outstanding as on a record date do not agree upon exercise of the right to realize their security under SARFAESI Act, s.22 of SICA will continue to have play.

Where, SARFAESI Act's uls.13 (9) is applicable for sick industrial company having more than one secured creditor or being jointly financed by secured creditors representing 60% or more in value of the amount outstanding as on a record date, such rights can be exercised.
cards.searchable_summary: The judgment clarifies that SARFAESI Act prevails over SICA to the extent of inconsistency. It also details how secured creditors can exercise their rights under SARFAESI Act when there are more than one secured creditors or jointly financed by them.

cards.case_title: THE POWER GRID CORPORATION OF INDIA LIMITED v. CENTURY TEXTILES & INDUSTRIES LIMITED
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the Power Grid Corporation has the right to erect power towers over a mining area leased by Century Textiles, and if so, whether it can realign the proposed route of the transmission project.
holding: The Supreme Court held that the Power Grid Corporation has all the powers vested in a Telegraph Authority under the Indian Telegraph Act, 1885, including the power to eliminate any obstruction in the laying down of power lines.

Therefore, it was within its right to erect the towers over the mining area. Further, change in route alignment of proposed power line was unfeasible as out of 410 towers which were to be erected under the project, 408 towers were already erected when the writ petition was filed by Century Textiles.
key_principles: The Power Grid Corporation has all the powers vested in a Telegraph Authority under the Indian Telegraph Act, 1885.
Change in route alignment of proposed power line is unfeasible.
cards.searchable_summary: Supreme Court upheld the Power Grid's right to erect towers over mining area leased by Century Textiles. The court also ruled that changing the route was not feasible due to already erected towers.

cards.case_title: A Sayyed Ratanbhai Sayeed (D) Th. Lrs. & Ors. v. Shirdi Nagar Panchay 1
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the appellants, small scale shopkeepers located in the vicinity of Shirdi shrine, should be displaced from their shops due to public interest considerations.
cards.holding: The court held that the private interests of the appellants are pitted against the overriding public interest. The displacement would result in an overall dislocation in their lives and cannot be fully neutralized by existing buildings elsewhere. Therefore, the appellants have been uprooted from their present sites of business.
key_principles: Public Interest Overruling Private Interests
Rehabilitation and Compensation
cards.searchable_summary: The case involves small scale shopkeepers facing eviction due to public interest considerations in Shirdi, Maharashtra. The court held that the appellants' private interests are outweighed by the need for public development.

cards.case_title: K.V. PRAKASH BABU v. STATE OF KARNATAKA
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the appellant is guilty under Section 498-A and Section 306 of the Penal Code for causing his wife's suicide by mental cruelty.
cards.holding: The Supreme Court held that there was no evidence to prove any kind of cruelty or ill-treatment by the husband towards the wife. The suspicion planted in her mind due to rumors did not constitute an offence under Section 498-A, and therefore, the appellant is not guilty under Section 306.
key_principles: Mental cruelty must be proven as wilful conduct leading to suicide
Extra-marital affairs alone do not amount to mental cruelty
cards.searchable_summary: The Supreme Court upheld the conviction of the appellant for causing his wife's suicide by mental cruelty under Section 498-A, but found no evidence of such cruelty and thus acquitted him of Section 306.

cards.case_title: A. AYYASAMY v. A. PARAMASIVAM & ORS.
cards.core_legal_question: Whether an application under Section 8 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996 is maintainable when there are serious allegations of fraud.
cards.holding: The Court held that although the cases where there are serious allegations of fraud that in normal course constitute criminal offences and are complex in nature demand extensive evidence do not fall under arbitrable disputes, mere allegation of fraud does not nullify the effect of an arbitration agreement. In the present case, the allegations of fraud were not so serious which could not be decided by the arbitrator.
key_principles: Fraud allegations must meet certain criteria to render a dispute non-arbitrable
cards.searchable_summary: The Court held that while serious allegations of fraud may make disputes non-arbitrable, mere allegations do not. The present case involved allegations not meeting these stringent requirements.

cards.case_title: POOJA PAL v. UNION OF INDIA AND ORS.
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the High Court was right in dismissing the writ petition seeking investigation by CBI into the incident, given perceived involvement of State administration and police.
cards.holding: If investigation is neither effective nor purposeful nor objective nor fair, it would be the solemn obligation of the courts to order further investigation or re-investigation as the case may be, to discover the truth through its effective and competent investigation.
key_principles: Remedial intervention in criminal cases
Investigation by CBI
cards.searchable_summary: The appellant sought investigation by CBI into her husband's murder due to perceived involvement of State administration and police. The Supreme Court upheld the need for further investigation or re-investigation if initial efforts were ineffective.

cards.case_title: SRIKANT ROY & ORS. v. STATE OF JHARKHAND & ORS.
key_statutes: Jharkhand Superior Judicial Services (Recruitment, Appointment and Conditions of Services) Rules, 2001
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the High Court was correct in holding that since 50% of the posts were already filled up by promotees on merit-cum-seniority basis, the left over 50% vacancies should be filled by promotion through Limited Competitive Examination and by direct recruitment from the Bar.
cards.holding: The Supreme Court held that the amended rules providing for the ratio to be maintained between the promotees and direct recruits became effective on August 20, 2004 and had prospective application. The High Court was correct in holding that since the writ petitioners have participated in the earlier selection process of 2010 and not in the subsequent selection process conducted on the basis of Notification dated February 22, 2013 for the year 2012, they cannot be given any relief.
key_principles: Amended rules providing for the ratio to be maintained between promotees and direct recruits became effective on August 20, 2004
cards.searchable_summary: The case dealt with a selection process for Additional District Judges in Jharkhand. The Supreme Court upheld the High Court's decision that since the petitioners had not participated in the subsequent selection process, they could not be granted relief.

cards.case_title: SATISH KUMAR v. KARAN SINGH AND ANOTHER
cards.core_legal_question: Whether a decree for specific performance can be passed on the basis of an executed receipt-cum-agreement when one condition in the lease deed makes the property non-transferable for ten years.
cards.holding: The Supreme Court held that Specific performance cannot be ordered if the contract itself suffers from some defect which makes the contract invalid or unenforceable. In this case, the High Court failed to hold that a decree for specific performance cannot be passed due to the condition in the lease deed making the property non-transferable.
key_principles: The validity of a contract is determined by its terms and conditions; if any term makes the contract invalid or unenforceable, it should not be enforced.
The discretionary power for granting a decree for specific performance must be exercised with due regard to all relevant facts and law laid down by this Court.
cards.searchable_summary: In this case, the Supreme Court upheld that a decree for specific performance cannot be granted when one condition in the lease deed makes the property non-transferable. The court emphasized the importance of considering all terms and conditions in determining contract validity.

cards.case_title: JUSTICE (RETD.) MARKANDEY KATJU v. THE LOK SABHA & ANR.
cards.core_legal_question: Whether a resolution passed by the Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha condemning derogatory remarks made by a retired Supreme Court judge on Facebook against Mahatma Gandhi and Subhash Chandra Bose is maintainable in a writ petition seeking quashing of such resolutions.
holding: Though Parliament/Houses have freedom of speech, collective expression of opinions culminating in a motion or resolution by the House does not enjoy immunity from legal proceedings in Court.
The Constitution guarantees free speech and expression under Art. 19(1)(a), without any distinction regarding popularity or dissemination.
key_principles: Freedom of Speech
Collective Expression Immunity
cards.searchable_summary: A retired Supreme Court judge's derogatory remarks on Facebook against Mahatma Gandhi and Subhash Chandra Bose were condemned by the Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha. The court held that such collective condemnation is not immune from legal proceedings, upholding the right to free speech under Art. 19(1)(a) of the Constitution.

cards.case_title: IDBI TRUSTEESHIP SERVICES LTD. v. HUBTOWN LTD.
cards.court: KURIAN JOSEPH AND R. F. NARIMAN, JJ.
key_statutes: Code of Civil Procedure, 1908: Or. XXXVII, r.3 (unamended and as amended in 1976)
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the change in law post-1976 affects the plaintiff's right to secure monetary terms for leave to defend a suit even if the defendant raises triable issues.
cards.holding: The Court held that XXXVII has suffered a change in 1976, and this change has made a difference in the law laid down in Meclte/ec's case (pre-amendment). The position now is that the trial Judge is vested with a discretion which has to result in justice being done on the facts of each case. Thus, post-amendment even if the defendant raises triable issues, if a doubt is left with the trial judge about the defendants good faith or the genuineness of the triable issues, the trial judge may impose conditions both as to time or mode of trial.
key_principles: The discretion vested in the trial Judge post-amendment allows for imposing conditions on leave to defend a suit even if the defendant raises triable issues.
If a doubt is left with the trial judge about the defendants good faith or genuineness of the triable issues, the trial judge may impose conditions both as to time or mode of trial.
cards.searchable_summary: The court held that post-1976 amendments in XXXVII have altered the law regarding granting leave to defend a suit. The discretion now lies with the trial Judge who can impose conditions even if the defendant raises triable issues, provided there is doubt about their good faith or genuineness.

cards.case_title: UNION OF INDIA & ANR. v. MIS INDUSIND BANK LTD. & ANR.
key_statutes: Contract Act, 1872: c s.28 (unamended and as amended w.e.f 08.01.1997)
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the bank guarantees could be invoked within the extended period of three months as per contract or later due to amendment in Section 28.
cards.holding: The court held that the unamended Section 28 would apply as on January 31, 1996, which is the date of the agreement of bank guarantee. The amended Section 28 operates prospectively and does not affect the validity of the guarantees dated earlier.
key_principles: Subject matter of Section 28 being 'agreements', the unamended Section 28 would be applicable as on January 31, 1996.
Amended Section 28 operates prospectively and does not affect the validity of the guarantees dated earlier.
cards.searchable_summary: The case pertains to a dispute between the Union of India and Mis Indusind Bank Ltd. regarding bank guarantees issued by the latter in January, 1996 for export of extra-long staple cotton. The court ruled that the unamended Section 28 applied as per the date of agreement (January 31, 1996), while the amended section operated prospectively.

cards.case_title: UCO BANK AND ANR. v. DIPAK DEBBARMA & ORS.
key_statutes: Securitisation and Reconstruction of Financial Assets and Enforcement of Security Interest Act, 2002
Tripura Land Revenue and Land Reforms Act, 1960
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the sale of mortgaged properties by a bank to any person who is not a member of a Scheduled Tribe is in violation of Section 187 of the Tripura Land Revenue and Land Reforms Act, 1960?
holding: The Court held that the Securitisation and Reconstruction of Financial Assets and Enforcement of Security Interest Act, 2002 (Act of 2002) enacted by the Union Parliament is referable to Entry 45 of List I, whereas the Tripura Land Revenue and Land Reforms Act, 1960 can be traced to Entries 18 and 45 of the State List.

In event of a conflict between the Central and State legislation, the dominant legislation (the Act of 2002) will prevail. Therefore, the provisions of the Tripura Land Revenue and Land Reforms Act, 1960, pro tanto, would be invalid to the extent it is inconsistent with the Act of 2002.
key_principles: Conflict of Laws
Dominant Legislation
cards.searchable_summary: The case involves a conflict between the Central and State legislation regarding the sale of mortgaged properties by banks. The Court held that the Union Parliament's Act of 2002, which allows for such sales without restrictions, conflicts with the Tripura Land Revenue and Land Reforms Act, 1960.

cards.case_title: MANBHAR DEVI AGARWAL v. THE STATE OF RAJASTHAN & ORS.
key_statutes: Rajasthan Mines and Minerals Concession Rules, 1986
cards.core_legal_question: Whether a contractor can be held liable for paying royalty on minerals used in construction work when the minerals are legally mined and proof of such legality is provided.
cards.holding: The Supreme Court upheld the High Court's decision to allow the appellant (contractor) to approach the mining engineer by written representation, providing details of amount deducted from its bills or withheld along with the details of minerals used. The court also held that circulars issued by the government required payment of royalty with regard to minerals used.
key_principles: The contractor can be exempted from paying royalty if they provide proof that the minerals used are legally mined and royalty has already been paid.
cards.searchable_summary: In this case, the Supreme Court ruled in favor of a contractor who argued that since the minerals were legally sourced and proof was provided, no further royalty should be payable. The court directed the appellant to approach the mining engineer for verification.

cards.case_title: M/S Gujarat State Fertilizers & Chemicals A Ltd. & Anr. v. Commissioner of Central Excise
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the payment made by GACL to GSFC constitutes a 'service' provided by GSFC for which service tax is chargeable.
cards.holding: We are of the opinion that the second ingredient has not been established in the present case and the question of service tax does not arise. In view thereof, it is not necessary to go into the question as to whether this payment constitutes a 'service' provided by GSFC for which service tax is chargeable.
key_principles: Joint venture between two parties sharing expenditure on handling facilities
cards.searchable_summary: The judgment clarifies that the payment made by GACL to GSFC does not constitute a service provided by GSFC, thus avoiding the applicability of service tax.

cards.case_title: BAIJNATH & OTHERS v. STATE OF MADHYA PRADESH
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the appellant can be held guilty under sections 304-B, 498-A and ss. 302, 201 and 34 of the Penal Code for dowry death.
cards.holding: The presumption as to dowry death gets activated only upon proof of the fact that the deceased lady was subjected to cruelty or harassment for or in connection with any demand for dowry by the accused. The High Court's reliance on statutory presumption u/s.113-B, Evidence Act, 1872 is misplaced since the prosecution failed to prove such fact by cogent, coherent and persuasive evidence.
key_principles: The presumption as to dowry death gets activated only upon proof of cruelty or harassment for or in connection with any demand for dowry.
If the prosecution fails to prove such fact, the person accused cannot be held guilty by taking refuge only of the statutory presumption u/s.113-B.
cards.searchable_summary: The High Court reversed an acquittal and upheld conviction based on a statutory presumption that was not supported by evidence proving cruelty or harassment for dowry demands.

cards.case_title: MANGANESE ORE INDIA LTD. v. STATE OF M.P. & ORS.
cards.court: [DIPAK MISRA AND SHIVA KIRTI SINGH, JJ.]
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the term 'processing' in the context of the Madhya Pradesh Electricity Duty Act, 1949 includes processes that result in a new or different article other than one which has been mined.
holding: The word 'processing' herein would mean those processes with help of hands or machineries connected and linked to mining activity. It would not include process by which a new or different article other than one which has been mined is produced.
Thus, tariff has to be levied as meant for manufacturing unit. Electricity Tariff - Interpretation of statutes - Maxim - Noscitur a sociis - Mines and Minerals.
key_principles: Noscitur a sociis
cards.searchable_summary: The judgment clarifies the interpretation of 'processing' under the Madhya Pradesh Electricity Duty Act, 1949. It holds that processing includes only those processes connected to mining activity and does not include production of new articles.

cards.case_title: STATE THROUGH LOKAYUKTA POLICE, RAICHUR v. C. N. MANJUNATH
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the respondent who was appointed as a licensed surveyor would be treated as 'public servant' for the purposes of Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988.
cards.holding: The Court held that once the nature of performance of duties gets crystallized, these licensed surveyors would come within the ambit of Section 2(c) of the Prevention of Corruption Act. In the duties that are to be discharged by these public surveyors, the State or the public has an interest therein.
key_principles: The statutory work of preparing survey sketches is bestowed upon licensed surveyors and they perform statutory duty/public functions.
These licensed surveyors are bound by the terms of their office as licensed surveyors, viz., the terms on which they are given license by the Government.
cards.searchable_summary: In this judgment, the Supreme Court clarifies that licensed surveyors who prepare survey sketches for government use and are governed by state-issued licenses fall under the ambit of 'public servant' for the purposes of the Prevention of Corruption Act.

cards.case_title: ARJUN GOPALAND ORS. v. UNION OF INDIA & ORS.
cards.court: [T. S. THAKUR, CJI, A. K. SIKRI ANDS. A. BOBDE, JJ.]
cards.core_legal_question: Whether allowing free trade in fireworks during the Diwali season constitutes a serious invasion of citizens' rights conferred by Part III of the Constitution.
cards.holding: Fireworks release deadly fumes into almost-still winter air, causing extreme air pollution and severe health effects. The balance must heavily tilt towards protecting present and future generations from harmful air quality over immediate commercial constraints.
key_principles: Protection of constitutional rights
Prioritization of public health over commercial interests
cards.searchable_summary: The case concerns the right to clean air in NCR, where fireworks release deadly fumes during Diwali season. The court held that allowing free trade in fireworks endangers citizens' health and must be heavily regulated.

cards.case_title: J. ASHOKA v. UNIVERSITY OF AGRICULTURAL SCIENCES & ORS.
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the Board of Regents of the University had power to select a candidate for appointment as Assistant Professor in Sericulture based on extraneous considerations against the appellant whose merit is superior.
cards.holding: The Board has the power to select the best candidate as per the provisions of the statute. Whenever the Board considers a person placed lower in merit in the select list, it can do so only by recording reasons as to why the case of the person placed above is being overlooked and the person below is considered the best for being appointed. On facts, the Board re-considered the matter and assigned cogent and adequate reasons for preferring respondent no.3, viz., her qualification, length of regular service as Assistant Professor and humanitarian grounds.
key_principles: The power to select candidates lies with the Board based on statutory provisions.
Reasons must be recorded when a lower-ranked candidate is selected over a higher-ranked one.
cards.searchable_summary: In this case, the appellant challenged the selection of respondent no.3 for appointment as Assistant Professor in Sericulture by the University of Agricultural Sciences. The Supreme Court upheld the Board's decision, emphasizing that reasons must be provided when selecting candidates lower in merit.

cards.case_title: REENA BANERJEE & ANR. v. GOVT. OF NCT OF DELHI & ORS.
cards.court: [T. S. THAKUR, CJI AND A. M. KHANWILKAR, J.]
key_statutes: Persons with Disabilities (Equal Opportunities, Protection of Rights and Full Participation) Act, 1995
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the High Court's direction to convene a joint meeting and submit a proposal about the reforms required in Asha Kiran Home was sufficient to address the deficiencies highlighted by the writ petitioners.
holding: The High Court directed the parties to convene a joint meeting and submit a proposal about the reforms required in the home, which the Delhi Government agreed to consider. The court further clarified that authorities should consider the suggestions made by the writ petitioner.

The Supreme Court affirmed the High Court's direction, emphasizing that the proposed action in the report would improve the condition of the home and hoped for implementation within an assured time frame.
key_principles: Full Participation
Equal Opportunities
Protection of Rights
cards.searchable_summary: High Court directed parties to convene a meeting and submit proposals, while Supreme Court affirmed these directions. The Delhi Government agreed to consider the suggestions made by the writ petitioners regarding the management of Asha Kiran Home.

cards.case_title: SH AMA v. STATE OF HARYANA
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the conviction and sentence of all three accused for murder should be affirmed.
holding: On facts, statement of victim-deceased was recorded only after doctor certified him to be in fit state of mind. The dying declaration of victim-deceased does not record any unnatural things or exaggerated version of incident.
The dying declaration (Ex. PF/I) was properly recorded and rightly relied on by the Courts below for resting the appellant's conviction.
key_principles: Dying declaration is admissible if it is properly recorded and corroborated by other evidence.
cards.searchable_summary: Supreme Court affirmed convictions of all three accused in murder case, holding that their dying declaration was properly recorded and corroborated by testimony of PW-3.

cards.case_title: C.B.I. BANK SECURITIES & FRAUD CELL v. RAMESH GELLI & ORS.
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the modification made in the judgment is valid under the provisions of the Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988 and Banking Regulation Act, 1949.
cards.holding: The Court held that the modifications made by Hon'ble Mr. Justice Prafulla C. Pant are valid as they correct errors in the earlier judgments regarding Sections 46A of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949 and Section 7 to 12 of the Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988.
key_principles: Validity of modifications under specific statutes
Correcting errors in judicial decisions
cards.searchable_summary: The case involves a modification made by Hon'ble Mr. Justice Prafulla C. Pant to correct errors in earlier judgments regarding Sections 46A of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949 and Section 7 to 12 of the Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988.

cards.case_title: MEARS GROUP INC. v. FERNAS INSAAT A.S.
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the former Supreme Court judge appointed to act as a sole arbitrator in terms of the arbitration agreement is valid given the circumstances.
cards.holding: In the circumstances, a former Supreme Court judge was appointed to act as a sole arbitrator in terms of the arbitration agreement. No possibility of any settlement between the parties and hence, the appointment by a former Supreme Court Judge was held valid.
key_principles: Appointment of Sole Arbitrator
cards.searchable_summary: The case involved an arbitration petition where the respondent refused to appoint a sole arbitrator as per the agreement. The court appointed a former Supreme Court judge due to the inability to settle disputes between the parties.

cards.case_title: KUNDAN LAL & ANR. v. KAMRUDDIN & ANR.
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the appellants have a right to claim possession of the suit property based on their claimed tenancy and possession from 'S'.
holding: The Court held that respondent no.1 has produced receipts regarding payment of rent, which are related to the suit property in question.
On the other hand, the appellants have not provided any receipts connected to the suit property, thus they do not have a right to claim possession of the suit property.
key_principles: The Court emphasized the importance of documentary evidence and receipt production in establishing claims of possession.
The Court also noted that the appellants' claimed tenancy from 'S' was not supported by any concurrent findings of fact, leading to their dismissal of appeal.
cards.searchable_summary: In this case, the Supreme Court dismissed an appeal where the appellants argued they had a right to possession based on their claim as tenants. The Court found that respondent no.1 produced valid receipts related to the suit property, while the appellants' claims were not supported by concurrent findings of fact.

cards.case_title: STATE OF JAMMU AND KASHMIR & ORS v. DISTRICT BAR ASSOCIATION, BANDIPORA
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the High Court erred in directing the regularization of two hundred nine daily rated workers without considering relevant constitutional and legal principles.
cards.holding: The High Court proceeded without considering relevant constitutional and legal principles enunciated in the judgments of the Supreme Court regarding the regularization of employees. The directions issued were totally unconnected to the reliefs sought in the PIL, and thus, the impugned order is set aside.
key_principles: Regularization of Employees
Constitutional and Legal Principles
cards.searchable_summary: The High Court improperly directed the regularization of daily rated workers without considering relevant constitutional and legal principles. The Supreme Court found this to be in error and set aside the order.

cards.case_title: STATE OF-UTTAR PRADESH AND OTHERS v. SUBHASH CHANDRA JAISWAL AND OTHERS
key_statutes: U.P. Excise Settlement of Licenses for Retail Sale of Foreign Liquor (excluding Beer and Wine) Rules, 2001
U.P. Excise (Settlement of Licenses Retail Sale of Country Liquor) Rules, 2002
U.P. Excise (Settlement of Licenses for Retail License for Model Shop of Foreig
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the High Court could issue general directions regarding the adequacy of sanctioned strength of police to maintain law and order in a case alleging violation of specific rules.
cards.holding: The High Court's issuance of such general directions was inappropriate as it fell outside its jurisdiction. The High Court should focus on adjudicating matters within the process of adjudication, not into legislative or executive domains. Therefore, the impugned order is set aside.
key_principles: Judicial restraint
Separation of powers between judiciary and legislature/executive
cards.searchable_summary: The Supreme Court overturned a High Court's general directions on police strength to maintain law and order in violation cases, emphasizing that courts should focus on adjudication rather than legislative matters.

cards.case_title: DELHI SUBORDINATE SERVICES SELECTION BOARD A v. PRAVEEN KUMAR
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the Appellant Board can decide on age limit for candidates in a manner that renders some eligible candidates ineligible.
holding: The employer's prerogative to decide the age limit and academic suitability of candidates is upheld, provided it does not contradict the academic eligibility as prescribed by the NCTE Act.
High Court's judgment in Sachin Gupta's case cannot be applied universally; it was a one-time relaxation for the year 2008 examination.
key_principles: Employer prerogative
Academic eligibility
cards.searchable_summary: The court held that the Appellant Board had the right to set age limits, but these must not conflict with academic eligibility as per NCTE Act. The judgment in Sachin Gupta's case is limited to its specific context and cannot be applied universally.

cards.case_title: BACHPAN BACHAO ANDOLAN v. UNION OF INDIA
key_statutes: United Nations Convention on the Rights of the Child
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the Union of India is obligated to formulate and implement a national action plan for children regarding substance abuse.
cards.holding: Protecting children from widespread prevalence of substance abuse is one of the biggest policy challenges facing India. The Union of India has an international obligation to curb drug abuse as a signatory to the United Nations Convention on the Rights of the Child. A comprehensive formulation of a National Plan/Policy is needed, forming the basis of coordinated intervention by the Union and State governments.
key_principles: International Obligation
Comprehensive Formulation of National Plan
Coordinated Intervention
cards.searchable_summary: The court held that India has an obligation to formulate and implement a national plan for children regarding substance abuse, citing its international commitments. The judgment emphasizes the need for comprehensive action by both central and state governments.

cards.case_title: VISHRAM @ PRASAD GOVEKAR & ORS. v. SUDESH GOVEKAR (D) BY LRS. & ORS.
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the respondents successfully discharged their onus of proving ownership by filing appropriate documents and whether relief for possession should have been sought is clearly untenable.
holding: The Supreme Court held that the respondents successfully discharged their onus of proving the ownership by filing appropriate documents, and dismissed the appeal against the dismissal of the suit for specific performance and mandatory injunction seeking demolition of the construction carried out by the appellants on the suit property.

In balancing the equities, the court allowed the relief of mandatory injunction seeking demolition of the construction carried out by the appellants on the suit property as it was illegally put up by them.
key_principles: The principle that courts should take note of events having a direct bearing on the relief claimed or the entire purpose of the suit and mould the relief accordingly, as observed in cases like Patterson v. Alabama and Lachmeshwar Prasad Shukul v. Keshwar Lal Chaudhuri.
cards.searchable_summary: The Supreme Court upheld the respondents' successful proof of ownership and allowed their mandatory injunction to demolish illegal constructions on the suit property, balancing equities in a property dispute between siblings and their children-in-law.

cards.case_title: GREAVES COTTON LIMITED v. UNITED MACHINERY AND APPLIANCES
cards.core_legal_question: Whether merely moving an application seeking further time to file written statement without replying to the allegations of the plaint would amount to making first statement on the substance of the dispute.
holding: Merely moving an application seeking further time to file written statement without replying to the allegations of the plaint does not amount to making a first statement on the substance of the dispute.

It does not appear from the language of Section 8(1) of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996 that the legislature intended to include a step like filing an application for extension of time to file written statement as a first statement.
key_principles: The High Court's decision on the application under Section 8 of the 1996 Act did not look into questions such as whether there is an agreement between the parties, whether disputes fall within the scope of arbitration, and whether the reliefs sought in the suit are those that can be adjudicated and granted in arbitration.
cards.searchable_summary: The High Court dismissed the appellant's application under Section 8 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996 for referring disputes to an arbitral tribunal. The court held that merely seeking further time to file a written statement without replying to the allegations in the plaint did not constitute making a first statement on the substance of the dispute.

cards.case_title: VATSALA SHENOY v. JOINT COMMISSIONER OF INCOME TAX, MYSORE
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the income from the sale of assets of a dissolved partnership firm is taxable as capital gains in the hands of former partners.
cards.holding: The court held that the result of winding up proceedings was to sell the assets of the firm and distribute the share thereof. Since the asset sold was a capital asset within the meaning of Section 2(14) of the Act, any gain arising from such transfer is treated as capital gains under Section 45. Therefore, the tax on the capital gains would be payable by the assessees.
key_principles: Capital Asset
Taxation of Capital Gains
cards.searchable_summary: The Supreme Court ruled that income from the sale of assets in a dissolved partnership firm is taxable as capital gains. The court held that since the asset sold was a capital asset, any gain arising from its transfer would be taxed under Section 45.

cards.case_title: VIJAY SHANKAR MISHRA v. UNION OF INDIA & ORS.
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the appellant's discharge from service and denial of disability pension were justified based on unsatisfactory conduct and six red ink entries in his service record.
cards.holding: The Court held that there was no application of mind by the authorities to consider other relevant circumstances, including the nature of the violation leading to the red ink entries, whether the appellant had been exposed to duty in hard stations or difficult conditions, and long years of service. Therefore, it is held that since the appellant would have attained the age of superannuation, he should be treated as having served until he could complete the qualifying period for pension.
key_principles: Application of mind by authorities
Consideration of other relevant circumstances
Age of superannuation
cards.searchable_summary: The Court ruled that the appellant's discharge from service and denial of disability pension were unjustified due to insufficient consideration of his service record, including hard stations exposure and long years of service. The ends of justice are met by treating him as having served until he could qualify for pension.

cards.case_title: SATYA PAL ANAND v. STATE OF M.P. & ORS.
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the appellant (son of the allottee) is entitled to be recognized as the owner of the plot after the Housing Co-operative Society executed a Deed of Extinguishment unilaterally and subsequently allotted the plot to respondent No.5.
cards.holding: The Court held that the appellant cannot be recognized as the owner of the plot because the Society's unilateral action in executing the Deed of Extinguishment was not valid, and the subsequent allotment by the Society to respondent No.5 did not create a legal right for the appellant.
key_principles: The validity of an allotment made by a Housing Co-operative Society is subject to compliance with its bye-laws
cards.searchable_summary: In this case, the Court addressed whether the appellant could claim ownership over a plot after the Society executed a unilateral Deed of Extinguishment and subsequently allotted it to respondent No.5.

cards.case_title: ANANTHESH BHAKTA REPRESENTED BY MOTHER USHA A.BHAKTA & ORS. v. NAYANA S. BHAKTA & ORS.
cards.court: R. K. AGRAWAL AND ASHOK BHUSHAN, JJ.)
cards.core_legal_question: Whether an application for reference of parties to arbitration can be entertained without the original or certified copy of the arbitration agreement.
cards.holding: The Court held that the Application for reference of parties to arbitration cannot be considered unless accompanied by the original arbitration agreement or a duly certified copy thereof. However, filing the application without such original or certified copy but bringing the original arbitration agreement on record at the time when the Court is considering the application shall not entail rejection of the application.
key_principles: The Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996: s.8(2) has to be interpreted to mean that the Court shall not consider any application for reference of parties to arbitration unless accompanied by original arbitration agreement or a duly certified copy thereof.
cards.searchable_summary: In this case, the court ruled on an application seeking reference of parties to arbitration under the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996. The Court held that such applications cannot be entertained without the original or certified copy of the arbitration agreement.

cards.case_title: A AMARSANG NATHAJI AS HIMSELF AND AS KARTA AND B MANAGER v. HARDIK HARSHADBHAI PATEL AND OTHERS
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the High Court followed the procedure in making an opinion that it was expedient in the interests of justice to file a complaint against the appellant under Section 340 of the CrPC.
cards.holding: In the instant case, the High Court has not followed all the requirements under Section 340 of the Code of Criminal Procedure (CrPC) as settled by the Supreme Court regarding the formation of opinion on expediency to initiate an inquiry into any offence. The court needs to make a decision whether it is required in the facts and circumstances of the case to file a complaint, after forming such an opinion.
key_principles: Procedure under Section 340(1) of CrPC
Formation of opinion on expediency to initiate inquiry
cards.searchable_summary: The High Court failed to adhere to the procedure prescribed by Section 340 of the Code of Criminal Procedure (CrPC), leading to a complaint against the appellant not being filed in accordance with the law.

cards.case_title: ANJAN DASGUPTA v. THE STATE OF WEST BENGAL & ORS.
cards.court: [PINAKI CHANDRA GHOSE AND ASHOK BHUSHAN, JJ.]
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the High Court correctly held that the appellants were involved in the murder case and should be convicted based on re-appreciation of evidence.
holding: While entertaining an appeal against acquittal, if two views are possible on appreciation of evidence, the view in favor of the accused has to be given importance.

In case an order of acquittal has been made on improper and erroneous appreciation of evidence, it is always open to the court of appeal to make proper and reasonable appreciation of evidence and differ from the order of acquittal.
key_principles: Re-appreciation of evidence
Proportionality in judicial decisions
cards.searchable_summary: The High Court upheld the conviction of the appellants for murder, reversing the trial court's acquittal based on a re-evaluation of the evidence. The judgment emphasizes the importance of considering all possible interpretations when deciding guilt or innocence.

cards.case_title: GOVT. OF NCT OF DELHI AND ANR. v. ANAND ARYA AND ORS.
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the construction of a bus depot on an area situated next to Nizamuddin Bridge and behind J.P. Power Station is permissible under the Master Plan 2021.
cards.holding: The High Court had given DTC (Delhi Transport Corporation) a chance to amend the Master Plan as per law if it was permissible, failing which the Millennium Bus Depot would be removed from the site. The Supreme Court upheld this decision and held that the development in recent times, particularly the orders of the National Green Tribunal, point out to the fact that things are moving towards determining whether the area is viable for the purpose or not.
key_principles: Permissibility under Master Plan 2021
Consideration of recent developments and expert committee's study
cards.searchable_summary: The case involves a dispute over the construction of a bus depot in Delhi. The Supreme Court upheld the High Court's decision to remove the bus depot, considering recent orders from the National Green Tribunal and an Expert Committee's findings.

cards.case_title: GOA FOUNDATION & ANR. v. STATE OF GOA & ANR.
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the Land Acquisition (Goa Amendment) Act, 2009 is valid.
cards.holding: The Goa Amendment Act, 2009 is valid. There is no repugnancy between the Principal Act and the State Amendment. The State Amendment by bringing in sub-sections (6) to (9) of s. 41 invalidates clause (viii) of the agreement by effecting a deletion thereof with retrospective effect.
key_principles: Rule of Law
Judicial Review
cards.searchable_summary: The court held that the Goa Amendment Act, 2009 is valid and does not conflict with the Principal Act. The amendment invalidates clause (viii) of the agreement by deleting it from its terms.

cards.case_title: BOBBILI RAMAKRISHNA RAJU YADAV & ORS. v. STATE OF ANDHRA PRADESH REP. BY ITS PUBLIC PROSECUTOR
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the continuation of the criminal proceeding against appellants 2 to 6 is justified under Section 6 of the Dowry Prohibition Act.
cards.holding: Giving of dowry and traditional presents at or about the time of wedding does not raise a presumption that such property was entrusted and put under the dominion of the parents-in-law of the bride or other close relations so as to attract ingredients of Section 6 of the Act. In the instant case, since after marriage, the deceased and first appellant resided in Bangalore while appellants 2 to 6 were residing separately in Vizianagaram, it cannot be said that the dowry was given to them and they were duty-bound to return the same.
key_principles: Dowry Prohibition Act
cards.searchable_summary: The Supreme Court held that dowry presents at or about the time of marriage do not automatically make parents-in-law liable for returning such gifts. The case involved appellants who faced criminal proceedings under Section 6 of the Dowry Prohibition Act, but the court found insufficient grounds to proceed against them.

cards.case_title: ROYAL WESTERN INDIA TURF CLUB LTD. v. E.S.I. CORPORATION & ORS.
cards.core_legal_question: Whether 'casual workers' would be covered by the definition of 'employee' under Section 2(9) of the Employees' State Insurance Act, 1948.
holding: The Court held that 'Casual Workers' would be covered by the definition of 'employee' in Section 2(9) of the Employees' State Insurance Act, 1948, based on Sections 2(22), 2(27) and Rules 2(2A) of the ES/ (Central) Rules.

The Court further held that the definition is very wide and includes various types of employees who are employed for wages in a factory or establishment on any work of, or incidental or preliminary to or connected with the work.
key_principles: Welfare legislation interpretation
Interpretation required to ensure extension of benefits
cards.searchable_summary: The Supreme Court ruled that 'casual workers' are covered by the Employees' State Insurance Act, 1948. The judgment emphasizes the wide definition of 'employee' and its applicability under various provisions.

cards.case_title: TEKAN ALIAS TEKRAM v. STATE OF MADHYA PRADESH (NOW CHHATTISGARH)
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the conviction and sentence of the accused for rape were valid given the prosecution's proof of guilt beyond reasonable doubt.
holding: The Court held that the prosecution has proved the guilt of the accused beyond a reasonable doubt. There was no infirmity or illegality in the judgment passed by the two courts, hence the conviction must stand in law.
The victim (now aged about 37 years) lives alone in her village and receives compensation of Rs.8000/- per month till her life time.
key_principles: Guilty beyond reasonable doubt
Compensation for rape victims
cards.searchable_summary: Supreme Court upheld the conviction and sentence for rape despite challenges. Victim awarded monthly compensation of Rs.8000/- by the State, with a provision to consider uniform scheme for future.

cards.case_title: SURESH NARAYAN KADAM & ORS. v. CENTRAL BANK OF INDIA & ORS.
key_statutes: Public Premises (Eviction of Unauthorised Occupants) Act, 1971
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the employees have a right to continue in the allotted premises since the allotment is not part of their condition of service.
holding: The Court held that if there is violation of the provision of lease deed between MHADA and the Bank, it is for them to settle their differences. Employees do not come into picture at all.
Employees have no made out case in their entitlement to continue in the allotted premises since the allotment is not part of their condition of service.
key_principles: Violation of lease deed between MHADA and Bank settles their differences
Allotment is not part of employees' condition of service
cards.searchable_summary: The Court held that employees do not have a right to continue in the allotted premises since the allotment was not part of their condition of service, and any damages claim against them by the bank must be settled between MHADA and the Bank.

cards.case_title: STATE OF M.P. v. UDAIBHAN
cards.court: Dipak Misra and Shiva Kirti Singh, JJ.
cards.core_legal_question: The High Court's decision to reduce the sentence from one year nine months to one year nine months already served is upheld by the court. The appeals are partly allowed and the sentence of rigorous imprisonment for three years in place of the period already undergone is imposed on all accused persons.
cards.holding: It is the duty of the court awarding sentence to ensure justice to both parties, and therefore undue leniency in awarding sentence needs to be avoided. The High Court's decision suffers from being overly lenient without any mitigating circumstances. The court alters the sentence to three years imprisonment for all accused persons.
key_principles: The duty of the court awarding sentence is to ensure justice to both parties.
Undue leniency in awarding sentence needs to be avoided as it does not have a deterrent effect on the accused and does not reassure society that the offender has been properly dealt with.
cards.searchable_summary: In this case, the High Court reduced the sentence from one year nine months to already served period. The appeals are partly allowed by the court, which alters the sentence to three years rigorous imprisonment for all accused persons.

cards.case_title: ASHOK KUMAR & ANR. v. STATE OF HARYANA
cards.court: KURIAN JOSEPH AND ROHINTON FALI NARIMAN, J.J.
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the court can award compensation beyond the amount claimed by the applicant in cases where properties do not abut the national highway and there is only a narrow road connecting them to the highway.
holding: The Court held that there is no restriction on the maximum amount of compensation awarded by the court, as the amendment in 1984 put a cap on the minimum amount of compensation. The current provision does not impose any cap on the maximum amount.
Therefore, it is clear that the amount of compensation that a court can award is no longer limited to what was claimed by the applicant.
key_principles: Just and fair compensation taking into consideration the true market value and other relevant factors
Awarding land value based on factual and legal position
cards.searchable_summary: The judgment discusses the Land Acquisition Act, 1894 and its amendments. It clarifies that courts can award compensation beyond what is claimed by applicants when properties do not abut national highways and there are only narrow roads connecting them.

cards.case_title: RE-INHUMAN CONDITIONS IN 1382 PRISONS
cards.court: Madan B. Lokur and R.K. Agrawal, JJ.
key_statutes: Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the conditions in prisons are humane and how to improve them.
cards.holding: Present order holds that prisoners deserve dignity and positive directions have been passed. The Undertrial Review Committee is directed to hold meetings periodically and follow up discussions with appropriate steps for release of undertrial prisoners and convicts. Member Secretaries of State Legal Services Authority and District Legal Services Authorities are also directed to ensure adequate assistance.
key_principles: Dignity of Prisoners
Effective Implementation of Laws
Utilisation of Funds
cards.searchable_summary: The court held that prison conditions need improvement, directing various authorities to take steps for better treatment and implementation of laws.

cards.case_title: HINA v. UNION OF INDIA & ORS.
cards.core_legal_question: Whether the rejection of allotment due to an age proof provided by the applicant being from a Higher Secondary School instead of Senior Secondary School is justified.
holding: The requirement of the Corporation is only a proof regarding the age. No doubt, certain documents are specified in the Eligibility Criteria which would be accepted by the Corporation as proof of age.

In case, a copy of the Secondary School Leaving Certificate can be accepted as proof of age, it does not even strike to common sense as to why the copy of the Higher Secondary School Leaving Certificate, duly attested, cannot be accepted as proof of age.
key_principles: The requirement for age proof is fulfilled.
The rejection of allotment based on an incorrect document type (Higher Secondary School instead of Senior Secondary School) is not justified.
cards.searchable_summary: In a civil appeal, the Supreme Court held that the rejection of allotment due to an applicant providing an age proof from a Higher Secondary School was unjustified and directed the Corporation to conduct fresh selection allowing the appellant's participation along with those already considered eligible.
